7 min read
Just now

--

Written by Jesse C — Founder @ Veilfire. Visit veilfire.io to see Safe, Secure, Ethical Artificial Intelligence.

Part 3 closed on a question. If your safety layer is something the agent can reach, what stops the agent from reaching past it? It is worth taking that question slowly, because the most natural answer in agent engineering is also the one that does not hold.
The natural answer is to give the agent a safety tool. A function it calls to ask whether an action is permitted. A guardrail library wired into the toolchain alongside the agent’s other capabilities. The appeal is real, and worth stating plainly. It fits the agent’s own pattern, where everything is a tool and the model decides which to call. It is easy to add, often a few lines. And it places the check right next to the action, where the context is freshest. For a first pass at safety, reaching for a tool is a reasonable instinct.
The problem is not how these tools are built. Many are built well. The problem is where they sit. If the safety check is something the agent invokes, then the agent’s own reasoning decides whether to invoke it, when, and what to do with the answer. And the agent’s reasoning is precisely the thing you cannot fully trust once an adversary is in the picture.
Security engineering named this gap fifty years ago. In 1975, Saltzer and Schroeder set out the principle of complete mediation: every access to every object must be checked for authority, every time [1]. The component that performs that check, the reference monitor, has three classic requirements, formalized in Anderson’s 1972 work: it must be always invoked so nothing bypasses it, it must be tamper-proof, and it must be small enough to be verified [2]. Hold a tool-based guardrail against those three requirements. The gaps that appear are not flaws in the guardrail. They are consequences of its position.
Take them as three ways the same control fails.
The first is the skipped check, and it breaks the always-invoked requirement directly. If the safety check is a tool, then calling it is one of the agent’s decisions. A well-behaved agent might call it before every action. But might is not must. The control runs through the model’s choice, and a model’s choices are probabilistic, not guaranteed. One skipped call is one action that reached the world unmediated. Complete mediation demands that the check happen on every action regardless of what the agent decided. A check the agent has to call cannot deliver that, because the calling is itself something the agent can fail to do.
The second is the check that gets talked around, and this is where agents break older assumptions most sharply. A language model processes instructions and data in the same channel, with no reliable separation between the two [3]. It cannot always tell “here is content to work on” from “here is a new instruction to follow.” In 2023, Greshake and colleagues showed what that enables. An attacker plants instructions inside the data an agent will later retrieve, a web page, a document, an email, the output of a tool, and the agent treats those planted instructions as its own, overriding the controls its operator set [4]. Now point that at a tool-based guardrail. If injected content can steer the agent’s behavior, the same injection that drives a harmful action can also suppress, mislead, or disarm the safety call meant to stop it. The guardrail and the thing it guards share one mind, and that mind can be captured. The danger is concrete: agents routinely hold credentials in the same context window as untrusted retrieved content, which is a straight path from injection to data exfiltration [4]. A safety layer living inside that context inherits its compromisability.
The third is the starved check, and it offends the principle of fail-safe defaults. Tools fail. They time out, they error, they rate-limit, they return something ambiguous. When the safety tool is just another call in the agent’s loop, what happens when it fails? Too often, the action proceeds, because nothing in the structure stops it. Saltzer and Schroeder’s fail-safe defaults principle says the default on uncertainty must be denial, not permission [1]. A guardrail the agent merely consults has no way to impose a safe default on the agent’s behalf. The agent is the one left holding a failed or missing answer, and the agent is the one that decides to keep going.
The thread running through all three is a single fact. The safety layer is downstream of the agent’s reasoning, dependent on it, and reachable by it. It is a subject being asked to police a peer, where the peer can choose to engage it, ignore it, or corrupt it. Complete mediation is violated by construction, because the mediator is something the mediated party controls. This is not an indictment of any product. It is a statement about geometry. A reference monitor cannot be a peer of the thing it monitors.
None of which makes in-agent checks worthless. As one layer among several they earn their place. A guardrail library can catch the obvious cases early and cheaply, close to the action. Defense in depth, another idea from the same era of security thinking, asks for multiple independent layers precisely so that no single failure is fatal, and an in-agent filter can be one of those layers. The mistake is not using them. The mistake is treating them as the floor. They are a layer. They cannot be the foundation, because the foundation has to hold in exactly the moment the agent does not.
So where does the foundation go? Out of the agent’s toolset, and into the execution path, above the reasoning layer, where the agent cannot reach it.
The shape is this. The agent reasons, plans, and proposes an action. Before that action takes effect, it passes through a checkpoint the agent neither controls nor can skip. There are three such checkpoints, one before the model is invoked on incoming input, one before any external tool runs, and one after the model produces an output. The agent’s model may decide to do something, but the doing is gated by an enforcer that sits outside the agent’s decision loop. The enforcer is not in the agent’s list of tools. It is in the path every action has to travel.
Placed there, the enforcer can meet the reference-monitor criteria that a tool never could. It is always invoked, because the runtime routes every external action through the gate, not because the agent remembered to call it. It is tamper-proof in the way that matters, because the agent has no access to the policy store that defines the rules or to the audit log that records the verdicts, and so cannot rewrite the constraints that bind it. And it is kept deliberately separable, so the part of the system you must trust is small enough to reason about.
This placement only works if all external authority is actually routed through the checkpoint. The agent cannot hold raw production credentials, open arbitrary network egress, call tools outside the broker, or write directly to protected resources. Complete mediation is a system property, not a library property. The runtime, credential model, network path, and tool broker all have to agree that the checkpoint is the only path outward.
This is what bounded autonomy means in practice. The agent is free to reason, plan, and act within its boundaries, and it cannot widen those boundaries on its own. It cannot modify the policies that govern it. Changing those rules requires human authority, and the change is recorded. The line of authority runs one way, from external standards, to organization policy, to the policy engine, to the agent’s operations, and never back up the chain. The agent operates inside the box. It does not get to redraw the box.
There is a temptation, when the enforcer needs to integrate cleanly with an agent framework, to make it just another tool the agent calls. That temptation has to be refused, because the moment the check becomes something the agent invokes, complete mediation is gone and every failure mode above returns. The placement above the reasoning layer is not an implementation convenience that could be traded for a tidier interface. It is the property that makes the enforcer an enforcer.
It is worth noticing that this is the same checkpoint from the previous part. The gate that enforces the rules is the gate that extracts the de-identified features, evaluates them, and returns a verdict of allow, block, or defer. Hard enforcement and privacy-preserving telemetry are not two systems. They are one checkpoint doing both jobs: proving the behavior without exposing the content, and applying the rule without asking the agent’s permission.
Position solves the integrity problem. It guarantees that the rules are applied, on every action, beyond the agent’s reach. But position alone does not say what the enforcer does when a rule fires, how it records the decision so the record can be trusted long after the fact, or how a human enters the loop when a decision needs judgment rather than a rule. Those are the working parts of assurance, and they are what the next part sets out.
References
[1] J. H. Saltzer and M. D. Schroeder, “The Protection of Information in Computer Systems,” Proceedings of the IEEE, vol. 63, no. 9, pp. 1278–1308, Sep. 1975. [Online]. Available: https://www.cs.virginia.edu/~evans/cs551/saltzer/
[2] J. P. Anderson, “Computer Security Technology Planning Study,” U.S. Air Force Electronic Systems Division, Bedford, MA, USA, Tech. Rep. ESD-TR-73–51, 1972. (Origin of the reference monitor concept.), [Online]. Available: Volume I https://seclab.cs.ucdavis.edu/projects/history/papers/ande72a.pdf and Volume II https://seclab.cs.ucdavis.edu/projects/history/papers/ande72.pdf
[3] OWASP, “OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications (2025),” OWASP GenAI Security Project, 2025; see “LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection.” [Online]. Available: https://genai.owasp.org/llm-top-10/ and https://genai.owasp.org/llmrisk/llm01-prompt-injection/
[4] K. Greshake, S. Abdelnabi, S. Mishra, C. Endres, T. Holz, and M. Fritz, “Not What You’ve Signed Up For: Compromising Real-World LLM-Integrated Applications with Indirect Prompt Injection,” in Proc. 16th ACM Workshop on Artificial Intelligence and Security (AISec ‘23), Copenhagen, Denmark, 2023, pp. 79–90. doi: 10.1145/3605764.3623985. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1145/3605764.3623985
